item_text: Public programs should preserve meaningful individual choice whenever possible.
Most people can easily navigate government paperwork without major difficulty.
Public agencies generally have legitimate reasons for requiring procedures in public programs.
When voluntary measures are insufficient, it is legitimate for the government to impose penalties to achieve public health goals.
When public health is at stake, individuals have a civic duty to comply with reasonable administrative procedures.
Individual privacy concerns should sometimes yield to legitimate public administrative needs.
Forms, waiting times, and documentation requirements can significantly reduce access to public services.
Bureaucratic procedures are more about protecting agency convenience than serving citizens.
Mandates and enforcement mechanisms tend to backfire by undermining trust and motivation.
Individual convenience should not be sacrificed for collective benefits.
It is acceptable for public programs to limit individual discretion when doing so improves administrative efficiency.
Public health agencies can generally be trusted to make decisions that protect the well-being of the population.
Even small procedural barriers can deter eligible individuals from receiving public benefits.
Coercive policy tools are sometimes necessary to protect public health.
Individuals should not be expected to tolerate inconvenience for the sake of public health goals.
Policies that reduce personal choice raise ethical concerns even when they improve program performance.
Government administrative requirements are often arbitrary obstacles that discourage people from accessing services.
The inconvenience of administrative procedures is usually exaggerated.
People should be willing to accept some procedural burden if it helps protect vulnerable groups.
Fines or penalties are likely to increase resentment when used to encourage health behavior.
Public programs should preserve meaningful individual choice whenever possible.
